name: Test React Components with Vitest and Testing Library
description: Set up and write component tests using Vitest and React Testing Library with user-centric queries and assertions.
trendingReason: Vitest has overtaken Jest as the fastest React testing framework with native ESM support

steps.title: Install Vitest and React Testing Library
steps.description: Add the testing dependencies including jsdom for browser environment simulation.

steps.title: Create the Vitest config
steps.description: Add a vitest.config.ts with jsdom environment and React plugin.

steps.title: Create the test setup file
steps.description: Import jest-dom matchers so you can use toBeInTheDocument() and similar assertions.

steps.title: Write a component test
steps.description: Create a test file that renders a component and asserts on its output.
steps.tip: Always query by role or accessible name instead of test IDs for more resilient tests.

steps.title: Run the tests
steps.description: Execute the test suite with Vitest in watch mode.
steps.tip: Use 'pnpm vitest run' for a single CI-friendly run without watch mode.

faq.question: Why Vitest over Jest for React projects?
faq.answer: Vitest is faster due to native ESM support, shares Vite's config and plugin ecosystem, and has a Jest-compatible API so migration is straightforward.

faq.question: How do I test components that use Next.js features like useRouter?
faq.answer: Mock Next.js modules with vi.mock('next/navigation', () => ({ useRouter: () => ({ push: vi.fn() }) })). For server components, test them as regular async functions.

faq.question: Should I test implementation details or user behavior?
faq.answer: Always test user behavior. Query by role, label, or text content. Avoid testing state variables or internal methods directly. If a user cannot observe it, do not test it.
